Lists activity records when MCPHub runs in database mode.
Returns a single activity record by id.
Reports whether activity endpoints are available on the current MCPHub instance.
Activity cleanup deletes history and therefore remains destructive.
Returns available filter values for the activity feed.
Returns aggregate activity statistics in database mode.
Authentication mutation routes are used by the REST client in JWT mode but are not exposed as management tools.
Bearer key listings are secret-sensitive and remain administrative.
Creating a bearer key changes control-plane access and remains dangerous configuration.
Deleting a bearer key is destructive for existing clients and remains dangerous configuration.
Updating bearer key properties changes privileged API access.
Authentication mutation routes are used by the REST client in JWT mode but are not exposed as management tools.
Authentication mutation routes are used by the REST client in JWT mode but are not exposed as management tools.
Returns the current authenticated MCPHub user.
Returns the resolved Better Auth user for the current request when Better Auth is enabled.
Returns MCPHub update metadata proxied from the changelog service.
Lists cloud market categories.
Lists cloud market servers for a category.
Lists cloud market servers.
Returns a single cloud market entry.
Lists callable tools for a cloud market server.
Calling a cloud market tool is intentionally reserved for the all profile because it crosses into downstream execution.
Searches cloud market servers by query string.
Lists cloud market tags.
Lists cloud market servers for a tag.
Returns context footprint cost data for groups.
Returns context footprint cost data for servers.
Lists MCPHub groups and their high-level metadata.
Creates a new group and changes routing behavior for downstream tools.
Deleting a group is destructive and must require explicit confirmation.
Returns a single group definition and summary metadata.
Updates group metadata and membership behavior.
Returns all per-server group overrides for a group.
Returns a single per-server group configuration override.
Updates group-scoped tool selection for a server.
Lists server membership for a single group.
Adds an existing server to a group without changing the server definition itself.
Removes a server from a group while preserving the server definition.
Batch replacement of group membership is operational but broad enough to require careful audit context.
Batch group creation is a broad infrastructure mutation and stays destructive.
Log clearing is destructive because it deletes audit evidence.
Returns MCPHub logs and must redact tokens, headers, and environment values.
Log streaming should be modeled as a future MCP resource subscription, not a request/response tool.
Lists market categories.
Lists market servers for a category.
Lists local market catalog servers.
Returns a single market catalog entry.
Searches market catalog servers by query string.
Lists market tags.
Lists market servers for a tag.
Settings export remains secret-sensitive and should only expose redacted content unless explicitly overridden.
These routes duplicate MCPHub downstream gateway behavior and are intentionally excluded from the management MCP surface.
MCPB upload accepts multipart content and remains dangerous configuration.
OAuth client management is administrative and secret-sensitive.
Creating an OAuth client introduces new credentials and remains dangerous configuration.
Deleting an OAuth client is destructive and changes auth posture.
OAuth client inspection remains administrative because secrets and redirect URIs require careful redaction.
Updating OAuth client configuration can break auth flows and is classified as dangerous configuration.
Secret regeneration is highly sensitive because it invalidates existing integrations immediately.
This OpenAPI document describes downstream MCP tool execution, not the management API itself.
Lists downstream servers visible to the OpenAPI generator.
OpenAPI generator statistics for downstream tool exposure.
Lists built-in MCPHub prompts.
Creates a built-in prompt exposed by MCPHub itself.
Deletes a built-in prompt and therefore requires destructive confirmation.
Returns a built-in prompt definition by id.
Updating a built-in prompt changes model-facing instructions and remains an operational write.
Lists MCP registry entries proxied by MCPHub.
Returns a specific registry server version payload.
Returns available versions for a registry server entry.
Lists built-in MCPHub resources.
Creates a built-in resource exposed by MCPHub itself.
Deletes a built-in resource and therefore requires destructive confirmation.
Returns a built-in resource definition by id.
Updating a built-in resource changes model-visible context and remains an operational write.
Reads built-in resource content and should stay read-only in the MCP surface.
Lists MCPHub server definitions with secret-bearing fields redacted in tool output.
Creates a server definition; stdio command-based servers remain gated by policy flags.
Deleting a server is destructive and must require explicit confirmation fields.
Returns a single server configuration with secret-bearing fields redacted.
Updates server configuration and requires destructive-style confirmation because a bad update can disable service access.
Reload is operational and reversible but still affects active sessions.
Enable or disable a managed server without deleting it; dry-run support should be preserved in the MCP layer.
Resets server prompt description overrides.
Prompt description overrides influence model-facing guidance and remain operational writes.
Toggles a downstream prompt inside a managed server definition.
Resets server resource description overrides.
Resource description overrides affect model context and stay in the ops profile.
Toggles a downstream resource inside a managed server definition.
Resets server tool description overrides to upstream defaults.
Description overrides affect agent behavior and therefore stay in the ops profile with audit context.
Toggles a downstream tool inside a managed server definition.
Batch server creation remains destructive because it mutates managed infrastructure at scale.
Returns a settings snapshot with secrets redacted by default in the MCP output layer.
System config writes can lock users out or weaken auth posture and therefore remain all-profile dangerous operations.
Exports a configuration template for operational reuse.
Exports a group as a reusable configuration template.
Template import can introduce arbitrary server definitions and remains dangerous configuration.
